title: Kelly Street Rezoning
description: The Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) is proposing to rezone three parcels in the Larimer neighborhood from R1D-L (Residential Single Unit Detached – Low Density) to UI (Urban Industrial). The site is bounded by Kelly Street to the north and Formosa Way to the south. Initial plans from the property owner propose a new multiunit residential building on the site, contingent on the rezoning. The UI district allows up to 60 ft height (4 stories), 3:1 FAR, and greater building densities.
keyDetails: Proposed by URA; rezoning from R1D-L to UI; future multiunit residential building planned; requires Planning Commission and City Council hearings; Development Activities Meeting held with Homewood Community Development Collaborative

title: Lower Hill District Housing RFP
description: The Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) is releasing a Request for Proposals (RFP) to redevelop 6.82 acres of URA-owned land on portions of Block A and Block C on the Lower Hill site, currently used as surface parking. The RFP will seek developers for affordable and/or market-rate housing, with additional consideration for mixed-use and commercial components. This is part of the broader 28-acre Lower Hill redevelopment.
address: Lower Hill District, Block A and Block C
keyDetails: 6.82 acres URA-owned land; public comment period open March 12 – April 7, 2026; URA Board vote on April 9, 2026; also includes Bedford Dwellings/Hill District mixed-income housing project (Trek Development Group + HACP) on 1.75 acres; funded in part by HUD Choice Neighborhoods Implementation Grant; $930,000 in Greater Hill District Neighborhood Reinvestment Fund grants also approved for four neighborhood projects (African Queens Apartments, Big Tom's Barbershop, Rhythm Square, Herron and Wylie Project)

title: Pittsburgh Comprehensive Plan
description: Pittsburgh's first-ever integrated citywide comprehensive plan, guiding growth and development across all 90 neighborhoods over a 20-year horizon. The plan will address future land use, zoning, climate action, housing, transportation, equity, and neighborhood planning. It must be approved by the Planning Commission and City Council.
keyDetails: Two-year planning process; community-led engagement with consultant team; guided by 'Just Transition' and climate justice principles; covers 8 themes: welcoming community, safe city, youth, economic opportunity, connected city, healthy residents, aging in place, thriving neighborhoods; Department of City Planning lead

title: Implementing the Housing Needs Assessment – Zoning Reforms
description: The Department of City Planning is implementing a suite of land-use zoning code reforms to increase housing options and affordability across all incomes. Completed reforms include minimum lot size reduction citywide (legislation 2025-1579 now in effect). Ongoing work includes accessory dwelling units (ADUs), inclusionary zoning, parking reform, and a comprehensive residential rezoning overhaul anticipated in late 2025/2026.
keyDetails: Minimum lot size legislation 2025-1579 passed and in effect citywide; future phases include comprehensive residential zoning code overhaul; inclusionary zoning (IZ) overlay currently active in Lawrenceville, Bloomfield, Polish Hill, portions of Oakland; parking reform complete; ADU expansion in progress

title: Mayor O'Connor Permitting Process Reform & Zoning Code Overhaul
description: Mayor Corey O'Connor announced a three-phase overhaul of the city's permitting and development operations on March 9, 2026. Phase 1 includes 20+ immediate actions: a 'fast lane' for quick permit approvals, more over-the-counter permits, triage by project size, AI-assisted application review, and virtual inspections. Phase 2 revises the zoning code (simplifying height requirements, updating UNC districts). Phase 3 consolidates online portals.
keyDetails: Announced March 9, 2026; executive order issued on mayor's first day requiring 60-day departmental review; first comprehensive zoning code rewrite in decades; Phase 1 actions being implemented Q1-Q2 2026; no citywide mandatory inclusionary zoning planned; targeted IZ near transit lines possible; DCP lead

title: California Ave. Bridge Rehabilitation Project
description: Rehabilitation of the California Avenue Bridge (Robert McAfee Bridge), which connects Brighton Heights and Marshall Shadeland and is a key roadway linking the North Hills to North Shore and Downtown. The bridge was built in 1928, rehabilitated in 1982, and is now rated 'Fair.' The project is currently in preliminary engineering (2024-2025), with final design planned for 2026-2027 and construction in 2028.
keyDetails: Bridge built 1928; eligible for National Register of Historic Places (Criterion C); community meeting held July 24, 2025; federal FHWA and PennDOT District 11-0 cooperation; consultants: MS Consultants (prime), Azalea Group, Monaloh Basin Engineers, GEO-Mechanics, Tri-State Design, Traffic Planning and Design; Timeline: Preliminary Engineering 2024-2025 → Final Design 2026-2027 → Construction 2028

title: Frankstown Avenue Signal Improvements
description: DOMI is making traffic signal improvements along Frankstown Avenue from Murtland Avenue to Blackadore Street in Homewood. The $4.3 million federally funded project (CMAQ grant) includes signal replacements at 4 intersections, signal removal/conversion to all-way stop at 2 intersections, curb extensions, ADA ramps, high visibility crosswalks, raised intersections, and speed humps. Construction began January 2026.
address: Frankstown Avenue, Murtland Ave to Blackadore St
keyDetails: Federal CMAQ grant of ~$4.3 million; construction began January 2026; 9 intersections in scope; part of 2022 Homewood Mobility Plan; DOMI lead; signal replacements at Murtland, Lang, Homewood, Collier, Brushton, Blackadore; signal removal at Braddock; all-way stops at Sterrett and Braddock; ADA improvements citywide

title: Liberty Avenue Highway Safety Improvement Project
description: A Highway Safety Improvement Program (HSIP) project to update roadway infrastructure along Liberty Avenue in the Strip District, improving vehicular and pedestrian safety. Includes full signal replacements at 16th, 21st, 25th, 26th, 28th, and 31st Streets, a new signal at 27th St, pedestrian bump-outs, refuge islands, improved lane widths, and transit improvements including widened bus stop sidewalks.
address: Liberty Avenue, Strip District (17th St to 32nd St)
keyDetails: HSIP (Federal Highway Safety Improvement Program) funded; full signal replacement at 6 intersections; new signal at 27th St; RRFB conversion at 17th St; part of Strip District Mobility Plan; also coordinates with Penn Avenue and Smallman Street corridor; transit signal priority under consideration

title: Brighton Heights, California & Termon Intersection Improvement
description: A study and design project to improve the intersection at Brighton Heights Boulevard, California Avenue, and Termon Avenue — a key gateway linking Brighton Heights to Ohio River Blvd (SR 65) and the McKees Rocks Bridge. From 2020–2024, nine crashes were reported at this intersection, four causing serious injury. The project will redesign signal timing, add protected turn phases, and improve pedestrian crossing conditions.
address: Brighton Heights Blvd / California Ave / Termon Ave
keyDetails: 9 crashes 2020-2024 (4 serious injury, 1 pedestrian injury); PRT Route 13 serves this intersection (~32 boardings/weekday); dominant crash type: 'angle' (turning/yielding behavior); project will produce implementable design and cost estimates but does not guarantee funding or immediate construction; DOMI lead

title: Penn Plaza Block B (Pennley Park South)
description: The final phase of the overall Pennley Park South site master plan at the former Penn Plaza site in East Liberty. Developer LG Realty Advisors, Inc. is advancing development plans for Block B following completion of Block A (Whole Foods, 2022) and Block C (renovated Enright Park). City Planning and Council District 9 are facilitating community engagement given the history of mass displacement of former Penn Plaza residents.
keyDetails: Developer: LG Realty Advisors, Inc.; privately-owned development (City role is zoning compliance review and engagement facilitation); Block A (Whole Foods) completed 2022; Block C (Enright Park renovation) complete; Block B is final phase; Council District 9 (Councilman Khari Mosley) involved; community questionnaire process underway

title: Bloomfield Bridge Rehabilitation
description: The City is rehabilitating the Bloomfield Bridge (completed 1986), which links the Bloomfield neighborhood to Downtown and Oakland. Noted as the largest deck-area bridge in Pittsburgh, spanning over the East Busway and railroad lines. The planned work will address pier deterioration and deck issues through substructure repair, bearing cleaning, spot painting, new deck overlay, and concrete approach repairs. Maintenance work on expansion joint seals was completed fall 2025.
keyDetails: Largest deck-area bridge in Pittsburgh; spans East Busway and railroad lines; emergency maintenance (expansion joint seals) completed fall 2025; full rehabilitation in planning phase; DOMI lead; work scope: substructure repair, bearing cleaning, steel spot painting, deck overlay, concrete approach repairs

title: Serpentine Drive Wall Repair & Access Conversion
description: Restoration of the historic retaining wall along Serpentine Drive in Schenley Park, where an 80-foot section collapsed in 2019. The project will repair approximately 265 feet of masonry wall with drainage improvements. Upon reopening, DOMI proposes Serpentine Drive be restricted to bike and pedestrian access only, eliminating motor vehicle access at both Barlett St and E Circuit Road ends.
keyDetails: Wall collapsed in 2019; ~265 feet of masonry wall replacement/repair; architectural treatment to replicate original stone masonry aesthetics; proposes closure to motor vehicles permanently; 60-day public comment period ended February 29, 2024; bike/pedestrian-only access planned; Council District 5

title: Warrington Recreation Center Renovation
description: Comprehensive renovation of the Warrington Recreation Center, a long-standing community hub. Interior improvements include a new recording studio, refreshed basketball and workout areas, warming kitchen, and an elevator for full ADA accessibility. Exterior improvements add a new ADA-accessible playground, outdoor basketball court, and improved site durability.
keyDetails: Funded by Redevelopment Assistance Capital Program (RACP) grants and local dollars; key features: recording studio, elevator (ADA), new playground, outdoor basketball court; Department of Public Works lead; project update posted January 2026

title: Vision Zero – Traffic Safety Program
description: Pittsburgh's citywide program to eliminate all traffic fatalities and serious injuries, adopted formally in 2024 following the city's Complete Streets Policy (2016). The program coordinates multiple agencies and roadway users using a safe systems approach focused on roadway design, education, and policy. It encompasses multiple active sub-projects including the Neighborhood Traffic Calming Program, Safe Routes to School, automated enforcement, and complete streets implementation.
keyDetails: Formally adopted 2024; Mayor Gainey's 2024 budget nearly doubled traffic safety capital investment; sub-programs include: Neighborhood Traffic Calming, Safe Routes to School, Automated Red Light Enforcement (ARLE), bike lane enforcement cameras, Complete Streets; DOMI lead

title: Vending Program Rules & Regulations
description: The City of Pittsburgh updated its mobile vending program through City Council legislation passed November 18, 2025 (Ordinances 34 and 35). The new rules and regulations governing the permitting process were anticipated to be published on March 12, 2026, upon which the new vending program would fully take effect. The project team was seeking public feedback on draft rules and regulations in early 2026.
keyDetails: Legislation passed November 18, 2025; signed by Mayor Gainey November 19, 2025; Ordinances 34 and 35; amended Zoning Code and Chapter 719 (Vendors and Peddlers); final rules/regulations publication target: March 12, 2026; PLI (Department of Permits, Licenses & Inspections) lead; covers stationary and mobile vendors in public right-of-way

title: Historic Preservation Program
description: Ongoing program by the Department of City Planning to protect and maintain significant landmarks throughout Pittsburgh. Guided by PreservePGH (Cultural Heritage Plan, 2012), the program includes establishing clear landmark nomination processes, Certificates of Appropriateness, and historic district design guidelines. Recent activity includes new design guidelines contracts for the Mexican War Streets and Manchester Historic Districts (funded March 2026).
keyDetails: Guided by PreservePGH (2012); ongoing Architectural Inventory Project (surveys since 2014); new design guidelines contract approved March 2026 for Mexican War Streets and Manchester Historic Districts (Evolve Environment::Architecture, $37,500); includes Hill District Pilot Process for Reuse/Reinvestment; DCP lead

title: South Side Park Project
description: Ongoing improvements to South Side Park, including Phase 1 renovations involving stormwater infrastructure (r-tanks maintained by Pittsburgh Water) and a new public art project funded by the Percent for Art program. City Council passed a resolution authorizing Pittsburgh Water access to the park to maintain stormwater r-tanks in March 2026.
keyDetails: Phase 1 ongoing; stormwater r-tanks installed in coordination with Pittsburgh Water (PWSA); public art project funded by Percent for Art program; City Council Resolution 2026-0177 authorized Pittsburgh Water maintenance access March 2026; Department of Public Works lead

title: Safe Routes to School Program
description: The Safe Routes to School (SRTS) program promotes students biking and walking to school through education, encouragement, and engineering safety improvements. Goals include educating school-aged children in safe walking and biking practices, encouraging active transportation, improving safety around crosswalks, signage, and sidewalks near schools, and partnering with community organizations citywide.
keyDetails: Ongoing annual program; DOMI lead; combines engineering improvements (crosswalks, signage, sidewalks) with education and encouragement; part of Vision Zero safe systems approach; updated January 2026

title: Pittsburgh Regional Disparity Study
description: The City of Pittsburgh is conducting a regional disparity study to understand the effects of race, ethnicity, and gender on the ability to do business throughout the city. The study examines whether there is disparity in the city's contracting and procurement, and will inform future Minority Business Enterprise (MBE) and Women Business Enterprise (WBE) program policies.
keyDetails: Citywide procurement disparity analysis; examines race, ethnicity, and gender-based disparities in city contracting; will inform MBE/WBE program policies; findings will guide equitable contracting requirements; Mayor's Office lead

title: Allegheny Riverfront Park Restoration
description: A $6.3 million restoration of Allegheny Riverfront Park's upper level between the Sister Bridges (Roberto Clemente, Andy Warhol, Rachel Carson) in Downtown Pittsburgh. Work includes replacing pavement, new site grading for accessibility, new amenities, refurbishing light posts and railings, tree pruning, soil amendments, and perennial planting. Led by Riverlife in partnership with the City and Pittsburgh Cultural Trust; original designer Michael Van Valkenburgh Associates involved.
address: Allegheny Riverfront Park (between 6th, 7th, and 9th Street Bridges)
keyDetails: Total project cost: $6.3 million; partners: Riverlife, City of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh Cultural Trust; designer: Michael Van Valkenburgh Associates + LaQuatra Bonci Associates; construction anticipated to begin 2024 (per earlier plans, may be underway); community meetings held December 2022 and February 2023; part of Riverlife 'Completing the Loop' vision

title: Westinghouse Park Master Plan
description: A community-led master planning process for Westinghouse Park (10.2 acres) in Point Breeze North and Homewood, formerly the estate of George and Marguerite Westinghouse. The plan incorporates gathering space, recreation amenities, ecological restoration, and stormwater management. The final plan has been published; implementation is the next phase.
keyDetails: 10.2-acre park; partnership between Westinghouse Park 2nd Century Coalition and Point Breeze North Development Corporation; funded by URA Neighborhood Initiative program grant and private funder; final master plan published; implementation phase upcoming; historically significant (Westinghouse estate 'Solitude')

title: In Progress
description: The Department of City Planning is reviewing the application for a new multi-unit apartment building in the Uptown Eco-Innovation District
